cards.a: High density shows more at once but risks feeling cluttered; low density feels calmer and clearer but shows less per screen.

cards.name: Responsive layout
cards.description: A layout that reshapes itself to fit any screen size, from phone to desktop. Elements reflow, resize, or stack rather than staying fixed.
cards.example: A three-column desktop layout collapses into a single stacked column on a phone so nothing is cut off or shrunk too small to read.
cards.q: What defines a responsive layout?
cards.a: It adapts to the screen size — reflowing, resizing, and rearranging elements — instead of holding one fixed arrangement.

cards.name: Reading flow
cards.description: The path the eye naturally travels across a page — often a Z shape for simple pages or an F shape for text-heavy ones. Designers place key elements along that path.
cards.example: A landing page puts its logo top-left, its call-to-action where the eye lands at the end of the Z, following how people actually scan.
cards.q: Why do designers care about reading flow?
cards.a: Because the eye travels a predictable path (like Z or F shapes); placing key elements along it ensures they are seen in the right order.

cards.name: Typeface vs font
cards.description: A typeface is the overall design of the letters (such as Helvetica); a font is one specific variation of it (Helvetica Bold, 12pt). The words are used loosely, but the distinction matters to designers.
cards.example: Saying 'I love this typeface' praises the whole family; 'use this font' picks one specific weight and size from it.
cards.q: What is the difference between a typeface and a font?
cards.a: A typeface is the letter design as a whole; a font is one specific size, weight, or style within that typeface.

cards.name: Serif vs sans-serif
cards.description: Serifs are the small feet or strokes at the ends of letters; sans-serif (without serif) letters are clean and plain. Each carries a different feel — traditional versus modern.
cards.example: A law firm uses a serif typeface to feel established and trustworthy, while a tech startup uses sans-serif to feel clean and current.
cards.q: What distinguishes serif from sans-serif type?
cards.a: Serif letters have small finishing strokes (feet) and read as traditional; sans-serif letters omit them and read as clean and modern.

cards.name: Type scale
cards.description: A graduated set of font sizes built on consistent ratios, used to create clear hierarchy. Sizes relate to each other deliberately rather than being picked at random.
cards.example: A design uses 12, 16, 24, 36, 54 for body to display text, so each level feels a clear step above the last.
cards.q: What is a type scale?
cards.a: A deliberate, ratio-based set of font sizes that creates consistent hierarchy between levels of text.

cards.name: Leading
cards.description: The vertical space between lines of text, named after the strips of lead once placed between lines. Too tight feels cramped; too loose breaks the text into disconnected stripes.
cards.example: A long article sets generous line-height so paragraphs feel airy and the reader's eye easily finds the next line.
cards.q: What does leading control?
cards.a: The vertical spacing between lines of text — affecting how readable and comfortable a block of text feels.

cards.name: Tracking & kerning
cards.description: Tracking is the uniform spacing applied across a run of letters; kerning is adjusting the space between specific letter pairs. Both fine-tune how text feels and reads.
cards.example: A logo gets manual kerning so the gap between 'A' and 'V' looks even, while a heading gets loosened tracking to feel elegant.
cards.q: How do tracking and kerning differ?
cards.a: Tracking adjusts spacing uniformly across many letters; kerning adjusts the space between two specific letters.

cards.name: Measure
cards.description: The width of a block of text, counted in characters per line. Around 45 to 75 characters reads most comfortably; too wide and the eye loses its place returning to the next line.
cards.example: A blog constrains its body text to about 65 characters wide even on a large screen, so reading never becomes tiring.
cards.q: What is 'measure' in typography and why does it matter?
cards.a: It is the line length (characters per line); roughly 45 to 75 characters reads best, beyond which the eye struggles to track lines.

cards.name: Font weight
cards.description: The thickness of a typeface's strokes, from thin and light to heavy and bold. Weight is one of the simplest, strongest tools for creating emphasis and hierarchy.
cards.example: A heading in bold and its body in regular weight instantly tells the reader which is more important, without any size change.
cards.q: How is font weight used in design?
cards.a: As a tool for emphasis and hierarchy — heavier weights draw attention and signal importance relative to lighter text.

cards.name: Type hierarchy
cards.description: Using size, weight, color, and spacing to signal the order of importance among pieces of text. It guides the reader from the most important element to the least.
cards.example: A page makes its title large and bold, its subheadings medium, and its body small and gray, so the eye knows exactly where to start.
cards.q: What is type hierarchy?
cards.a: The use of size, weight, and spacing to rank text by importance, guiding the reader's eye through the content in order.

cards.name: Hue, saturation, value
cards.description: The three dials of any color: hue is which color it is (red, blue), saturation is how intense or muted it is, and value is how light or dark it is. Thinking in these terms makes color choices deliberate.
cards.example: To make a brand color feel calmer, a designer keeps the hue but lowers the saturation, rather than guessing at a new color.
cards.q: What are the three properties of a color in the HSB model?
cards.a: Hue (which color), saturation (intensity), and value (lightness or darkness).

cards.name: Tints & shades
cards.description: A tint is a color mixed with white, making it lighter; a shade is a color mixed with black, making it darker. They are how a single color becomes a usable range.
cards.example: A brand's one blue becomes a whole palette of tints for backgrounds and shades for text, all clearly related.
cards.q: What is the difference between a tint and a shade?
cards.a: A tint is a color lightened with white; a shade is a color darkened with black.

cards.name: Palette roles
cards.description: In a working palette each color has a job: primary (the main brand color), secondary (support), accent (highlights and actions), and neutral (text and backgrounds). Roles prevent color chaos.
cards.example: A product uses one primary blue, gray neutrals for most of the screen, and a single orange accent reserved for buttons.
cards.q: What are the typical roles in a color palette?
cards.a: Primary (main brand), secondary (support), accent (highlights and actions), and neutral (text and backgrounds).

cards.name: Semantic color
cards.description: Color used to carry meaning rather than just decoration: green for success, red for error, yellow for warning. It communicates state at a glance.
cards.example: A form turns a field border red with an error message, so the user instantly knows something needs fixing.
cards.q: What is semantic color?
cards.a: Color chosen to convey meaning or state — such as green for success or red for error — not merely for looks.

cards.name: Color contrast
cards.description: The measurable difference in lightness between text and its background. Enough contrast keeps text readable for everyone, including people with low vision; too little makes it disappear.
cards.example: Light gray text on a white background may look elegant but fails contrast standards and is unreadable for many users.
cards.q: Why does color contrast matter beyond aesthetics?
cards.a: Sufficient contrast between text and background keeps content readable and accessible; too little excludes users with low vision.

cards.name: 60-30-10 rule
cards.description: A guideline for balanced color: about 60 percent a dominant color, 30 percent a secondary, and 10 percent an accent. It keeps a palette from feeling random or overwhelming.
cards.example: A room or a webpage uses mostly a neutral base (60), a supporting tone (30), and a small pop of bold color (10).
cards.q: What does the 60-30-10 rule describe?
cards.a: A balanced split of a palette — roughly 60% dominant color, 30% secondary, 10% accent — for visual harmony.

cards.name: Warm vs cool
cards.description: Warm colors (reds, oranges, yellows) feel energetic and seem to advance toward the viewer; cool colors (blues, greens) feel calm and seem to recede. Designers use this to set mood and depth.
cards.example: A sale banner uses warm red to feel urgent, while a banking app uses cool blue to feel calm and trustworthy.
cards.q: How do warm and cool colors behave differently?
cards.a: Warm colors energize and appear to advance; cool colors calm and appear to recede — shaping mood and perceived depth.

cards.name: Color schemes
cards.description: Reliable color relationships drawn from the color wheel: monochromatic (one hue's variations), analogous (neighbors), and complementary (opposites). They give a fast route to palettes that work.
cards.example: A designer picks two opposite hues for a complementary scheme to make a call-to-action pop against its background.
cards.q: Name three common color schemes from the color wheel.
cards.a: Monochromatic (variations of one hue), analogous (neighboring hues), and complementary (opposite hues).

cards.name: Contrast
cards.description: Making elements clearly different so they do not compete or blur together. The eye needs difference — in size, color, or weight — to know what matters and how to navigate.
cards.example: A big bold headline above small gray body text uses contrast so the reader instantly sees the structure of the page.
cards.q: Why is contrast a core design principle?
cards.a: The eye relies on difference to navigate; contrast in size, color, or weight makes elements distinct and signals what matters.

cards.name: Repetition
cards.description: Reusing the same visual elements — colors, fonts, spacing, shapes — throughout a design. It creates unity, rhythm, and a sense that everything belongs together.
cards.example: Every card in a feed uses the same corner radius, padding, and title style, so the whole screen feels like one coherent system.
cards.q: What does repetition achieve in a design?
cards.a: It creates unity and rhythm by reusing consistent visual elements, making a design feel coherent and intentional.

cards.name: Alignment
cards.description: Lining elements up to shared invisible edges so nothing feels arbitrarily placed. Strong alignment creates clean, calm, professional layouts.
cards.example: Form labels and fields share a left edge, so the form looks orderly instead of a scatter of loosely placed boxes.
cards.q: What does alignment contribute to a layout?
cards.a: It connects elements to shared edges so the design feels intentional and orderly rather than randomly placed.

cards.name: Proximity
cards.description: Placing related items close together and unrelated items farther apart. The eye reads closeness as relationship, so proximity groups content without needing boxes or lines.
cards.example: A caption sits tight under its image and far from the next one, so it is obvious which photo it describes.
cards.q: How does proximity organize content?
cards.a: By grouping related elements close together and separating unrelated ones, since the eye reads nearness as relationship.

cards.name: Visual hierarchy
cards.description: Arranging elements so the eye knows what to look at first, second, and third. It is created with size, color, contrast, and position working together.
cards.example: A poster's event name dominates, the date sits second, and the fine print is smallest — the eye reads them in that order automatically.
cards.q: What is visual hierarchy?
cards.a: The deliberate ordering of elements by importance, using size, contrast, and position to guide what the eye sees first.

cards.name: Balance
cards.description: Distributing visual weight across a layout so it feels stable. Symmetrical balance feels formal and calm; asymmetrical balance feels dynamic but takes more skill.
cards.example: A wedding invitation centers everything for formal symmetry, while a sports brand offsets a bold image with small text for energy.
cards.q: What is the difference between symmetrical and asymmetrical balance?
cards.a: Symmetrical balance mirrors weight evenly and feels formal; asymmetrical balance offsets different weights and feels dynamic.

cards.name: Emphasis
cards.description: Designing one element to grab attention first — the focal point. Without emphasis everything competes equally and the eye does not know where to land.
cards.example: A page makes its single 'Sign up' button bold and colorful so it is unmistakably the first thing the eye is drawn to.
cards.q: What is emphasis (the focal point) in a composition?
cards.a: The element deliberately made to stand out and capture attention first, giving the eye a clear place to start.

cards.name: Gestalt principles
cards.description: A set of rules describing how the brain groups separate parts into a whole — proximity, similarity, closure, and continuity. They explain why we see patterns rather than scattered pieces.
cards.example: Three dots arranged in a triangle read as a shape, not three separate dots — the mind completes the pattern (closure).
cards.q: What do the Gestalt principles describe?
cards.a: How the brain groups individual elements into wholes, through proximity, similarity, closure, and continuity.

cards.name: Figure & ground
cards.description: The brain instinctively separates a subject (figure) from its background (ground). Good design makes this separation effortless; ambiguous figure-ground confuses the eye.
cards.example: White text on a busy photo becomes unreadable because figure and ground blur; a dark overlay restores the separation.
cards.q: What is the figure-ground relationship?
cards.a: The mind's separation of a subject (figure) from its background (ground); clear separation makes a design easy to read.

cards.name: Visual rhythm
cards.description: A sense of movement created by repeating elements at regular intervals, like beats in music. It carries the eye smoothly through a design.
cards.example: Evenly spaced section breaks down a long page give it a steady rhythm, so reading feels paced rather than relentless.
cards.q: What creates visual rhythm?
cards.a: Repeating elements at regular intervals, which gives a design a sense of pace and movement that carries the eye.

cards.name: Design system
cards.description: A single source of truth for a product's design: reusable components, rules, and styles that keep everything consistent as a team and product grow. More than a style guide — it is the working toolkit.
cards.example: A company's design system means a new feature is built from existing buttons, cards, and colors, so it looks native on day one.
cards.q: What is a design system?
cards.a: A shared, reusable set of components, rules, and styles that keeps a product visually and behaviorally consistent at scale.

cards.name: Design language
cards.description: The shared visual personality and vocabulary that runs across a product or brand — its shapes, motion, tone, and feel. It is what makes different screens recognizably the same family.
cards.example: Every Apple product shares a design language of rounded rectangles, generous space, and restraint, so they feel related.
cards.q: What is a design language?
cards.a: The consistent visual personality and vocabulary across a product — shapes, tone, motion — that makes everything feel related.

cards.name: Design tokens
cards.description: Named values for design decisions — like color-primary or spacing-medium — stored once and reused everywhere. Change the token and every place that uses it updates at once.
cards.example: A brand darkens its primary color by editing one token, and every button across the app updates instantly.
cards.q: What is a design token?
cards.a: A named, reusable value (for a color, spacing, or font) stored once so a single change updates everywhere it is used.

cards.name: Atomic design
cards.description: A method of building interfaces from the smallest parts up: atoms (a button, an input) combine into molecules, then organisms, then templates and pages. It encourages reuse and consistency.
cards.example: A search bar (molecule) is made from an input and a button (atoms), and reused in many organisms across the product.
cards.q: What is the idea behind atomic design?
cards.a: Building UIs from small reusable parts upward — atoms to molecules to organisms to templates to pages — for consistency and reuse.

cards.name: Affordance
cards.description: A visual quality that suggests how something can be used. A raised button looks pressable; a slider looks draggable. Good affordances make interfaces feel obvious.
cards.example: A button with a subtle shadow looks clickable, while a flat shape with no cue leaves users unsure whether to tap it.
cards.q: What is an affordance?
cards.a: A visual cue suggesting how an element can be used — like a button that looks pressable — making interaction feel obvious.

cards.name: Signifier
cards.description: An explicit signal that tells the user where or how to act. Where an affordance hints at possible use, a signifier points it out directly — an underline, an arrow, a label.
cards.example: An underline under text signifies 'this is a link, click it,' making the affordance of the link explicit.
cards.q: How does a signifier differ from an affordance?
cards.a: An affordance is the possibility of an action; a signifier is the explicit cue (underline, arrow, label) that points the action out.

cards.name: Consistency
cards.description: Making the same things look and behave the same way everywhere. Consistency lowers the effort of learning an interface, because a pattern learned once works throughout.
cards.example: If every primary action is a blue button, the user never has to relearn what to tap on a new screen.
cards.q: Why is consistency valuable in design?
cards.a: It lets a user apply a pattern learned once across the whole product, reducing the effort of understanding the interface.

cards.name: Skeuomorphism vs flat
cards.description: Skeuomorphic design imitates real-world objects with textures, shadows, and depth; flat design strips those away for clean, simple shapes and color. The pendulum of taste swings between them.
cards.example: Early iPhone apps mimicked leather and paper (skeuomorphic); later ones went flat with plain color blocks and crisp type.
cards.q: What is the difference between skeuomorphic and flat design?
cards.a: Skeuomorphism mimics real objects with texture and depth; flat design removes those for simple shapes and color.

decks.name: Cloth & Thread
decks.tagline: The language of fine cotton and linen

cards.name: Staple length
cards.description: The length of individual cotton or linen fibers before spinning. Longer fibers produce smoother, finer, stronger yarn — they grip each other more tightly, pill less, and last longer. Staple length is the single most important quality indicator in raw cotton, and the reason cheap cotton deteriorates after a few washes.
cards.example: A mill quotes two cottons at the same price: one at 24mm staple, one at 34mm. You choose the 34mm — longer staple means the finished fabric will feel smoother, pill far less, and hold up through repeated washing.
cards.q: Why does staple length matter more than thread count when choosing cotton?
cards.a: Longer fibers make smoother, stronger yarn that pills less; thread count can be inflated by cheap short-staple yarn, but staple length cannot be faked.

cards.name: Extra-long staple (ELS)
cards.description: Cotton with individual fibers of 34mm or longer — roughly twice the length of standard upland cotton. ELS produces the finest, softest, most durable yarns and makes up only about 3% of the world's cotton crop. Egyptian, Pima, and Sea Island are all ELS cottons, which is why they command a significant premium.
cards.example: A vendor listing says 'ELS cotton, Ne 80 combed ring-spun.' Even before you see a swatch, those words tell you the raw material is premium grade — fine, strong, and from a rare segment of global production.
cards.q: What qualifies as ELS cotton and why does it command a premium?
cards.a: Fibers 34mm or longer; the extra length enables finer, stronger, longer-lasting yarn — and ELS accounts for only ~3% of global cotton production.

cards.name: Egyptian cotton
cards.description: Extra-long staple cotton grown in Egypt's Nile Delta, prized for its fine, lustrous fibers and exceptional softness. It is also the most frequently mislabeled cotton in the industry — vendors can claim 'Egyptian origin' for cotton blended or processed in Egypt without using Egyptian-grown fiber. Authentic Egyptian cotton carries a Cotton Egypt Association (CEA) certification.
cards.example: A vendor offers 'Egyptian cotton' bedding at a price that seems too low for ELS quality. You ask for the CEA certificate — they cannot produce one. The label was a marketing claim, not a certified origin, and the fiber is almost certainly a cheaper blend.
cards.q: How do you verify a claim of genuine Egyptian cotton?
cards.a: Ask for the Cotton Egypt Association (CEA) certificate — without it, 'Egyptian cotton' is an unverified label, not a certified origin.

cards.name: Pima & Supima
cards.description: Pima is American-grown extra-long staple cotton, comparable in quality to Egyptian. Supima is a trademarked certification owned by Supima (Supima cotton producers) that guarantees the cotton is genuine American-grown Pima, verifiable against their licensee database. Ask for Supima over plain 'Pima' when authenticity matters — any mill can label cotton as Pima without verification.
cards.example: A vendor claims 'Pima cotton' but cannot provide a Supima license number. You note the difference: Pima is a variety name anyone can use; Supima is a certified trademark with a verifiable license. You source from a Supima-licensed supplier instead.
cards.q: What is the difference between Pima and Supima?
cards.a: Pima is the cotton variety name — unprotected, any vendor can claim it. Supima is a certified trademark guaranteeing genuine American-grown Pima, verifiable through Supima's licensee database.

cards.name: Yarn count (Ne)
cards.description: A measure of how fine a cotton yarn is: the number of 840-yard lengths per pound of fiber. Higher Ne = finer, lighter, softer yarn. Ne 20–30 is workwear weight; Ne 40–60 is quality apparel; Ne 80–120 is luxury shirting. Finer yarns require longer staple fiber to spin without breaking, which is why fine Ne and ELS cotton go together.
cards.example: A vendor describes a fabric as 'Ne 40/2 combed ring-spun.' You read that as: a 2-ply yarn, each strand spun to Ne 40 fineness, from combed fiber, ring-spun — quality apparel weight. The notation tells you the full construction story.
cards.q: A vendor offers fabric in Ne 40 and Ne 80. Which is finer and what does that imply?
cards.a: Ne 80 is finer — more 840-yard lengths per pound. It produces softer, lighter fabric but requires longer-staple cotton to spin reliably, which is why fine Ne fabrics cost more.

cards.name: Combed cotton
cards.description: Combing is a secondary process after carding that removes short fibers and impurities, leaving only the longest, most uniform fibers, aligned parallel. Combed cotton produces finer, smoother, stronger yarn that takes dye more evenly. In quality organic fabric, always specify combed — the difference is noticeable in both hand feel and how the fabric ages.
cards.example: Two organic certifications are otherwise identical: one specifies 'combed ring-spun,' the other simply 'ring-spun.' You ask — the second is carded only. The combed version will pill markedly less and feel distinctly softer over years of washing.
cards.q: What does specifying combed cotton buy you over carded-only cotton?
cards.a: Short fibers and impurities are removed, leaving only long, uniform fibers — the yarn is finer, smoother, stronger, and takes dye more evenly than carded-only cotton.

cards.name: Ring-spun yarn
cards.description: Ring spinning slowly draws and twists fibers together, producing a tight, smooth, strong yarn. Open-end (rotor) spinning is faster and cheaper but yields a bulkier, coarser, weaker thread. For quality fabric, always specify ring-spun. A vendor listing 'OE cotton' is signaling a cost-optimized, not quality-optimized, construction.
cards.example: A mill offers two options: 'OE poplin' and 'ring-spun combed poplin' at a higher price. OE stands for open-end — faster and cheaper to produce, noticeably coarser to the hand. The premium for ring-spun is real and worth it for a quality brand.
cards.q: Why specify ring-spun over open-end (OE) yarn?
cards.a: Ring spinning produces finer, smoother, stronger yarn by slowly twisting aligned fibers; open-end is faster and cheaper but makes a coarser, weaker thread that shows it over time.

cards.name: Singles vs ply yarn
cards.description: A singles yarn is one twisted strand (notated as /1). A 2-ply twists two singles together for greater strength and evenness (/2). A notation like '40/2' means Ne 40 singles twisted into 2-ply. Ply yarn is stronger, more uniform, and develops fewer thin spots over time — standard in quality shirting and percale bedding intended for long-term use.
cards.example: A vendor's listing shows '40/1 combed' at a lower price and '40/2 combed' at a premium. The /2 means two-ply — heavier, more durable, less likely to develop thin spots or pilling. For products built to last, /2 is the right choice.
cards.q: What does '40/2' mean on a yarn specification?
cards.a: Ne 40 singles yarn twisted into a 2-ply thread — two strands twisted together for greater strength, uniformity, and durability than a single /1 strand of the same fineness.

cards.name: Linen / flax
cards.description: Linen is a bast fiber extracted from the stem of the flax plant. Naturally antibacterial, highly breathable, temperature-regulating, and extremely strong — it gets noticeably softer with each wash. European flax (Belgium, France, Netherlands) is the quality benchmark: the region's wet climate produces longer, finer, more uniform fiber. Chinese flax is more common and cheaper, but less consistent in quality.
cards.example: Two vendors offer certified organic linen: one Belgian origin, one Chinese. You ask both for fiber uniformity data and retting method. The Belgian supplier provides water-retting documentation and CEL certification (Confédération Européenne du Lin); the difference in hand feel on the swatches confirms the origin premium.
cards.q: What makes European flax linen different from Chinese flax?
cards.a: European flax — particularly Belgian — grows in a climate producing longer, finer, more uniform fiber; CEL (European Linen) certification is the benchmark. Chinese flax is cheaper but quality is less consistent.

cards.name: Retting
cards.description: The process of breaking down the plant matter around flax stems to release the bast fibers. Water retting — submerging stems in still water or dedicated pools — produces the finest, most uniform fiber and is the traditional Belgian method. Dew retting (laying stems in fields) is slower and produces coarser, less uniform fiber. Chemical retting is the fastest option but damages fiber quality and is not compatible with organic certification.
cards.example: You ask a Belgian linen vendor: 'What retting method?' They answer 'water retted.' That one fact tells you the fibers will be more uniform and finer than a dew-retted alternative — it's a meaningful quality signal before you even see a swatch.
cards.q: Why does water retting produce better linen fiber than dew retting?
cards.a: Submerging flax in water produces a controlled, even decomposition that yields finer, more uniform fiber; dew retting is slower and less controlled, producing coarser results and more variation.

cards.name: GSM
cards.description: The weight of a fabric per square metre — the universal standard for comparing substance across different constructions. Lighter GSM is airier and more transparent; heavier GSM is more substantial and opaque. Reference points: voile is ~60–80 GSM; quality shirting cotton is 110–140 GSM; luxury bedding linen is 190–230 GSM; upholstery fabric is 400+ GSM. Always specify your target GSM and acceptable tolerance in your purchase order.
cards.example: A vendor sends two organic linen swatches labeled identically but at different prices. You check the spec sheets: one is 155 GSM, the other 210 GSM. The heavier will feel noticeably more substantial — you need your end-use in mind before deciding which is right.
cards.q: Why specify a GSM target in your purchase order rather than just a fabric description?
cards.a: GSM is the objective measure of fabric substance — it sets a testable spec so you can reject a shipment that comes in significantly lighter or heavier than what you approved at sampling.

cards.name: Thread count
cards.description: The number of warp and weft threads per square inch in woven fabric. It is a meaningful quality indicator up to roughly 400; above that, manufacturers routinely use multi-ply yarns and count each strand individually to inflate the number. A 600-count sheet woven from short-staple 2-ply yarn can feel worse than a 300-count sheet from long-staple combed cotton. Staple length, yarn quality, and weave matter far more.
cards.example: A supplier promotes '800-thread-count Egyptian cotton.' You ask: 'What is the staple length, and is the count based on singles or ply yarns?' They cannot answer. That inability is the red flag — a meaningful thread count claim comes with full yarn specifications behind it.
cards.q: Why can a 300-thread-count sheet feel better than a 600-thread-count sheet?
cards.a: Thread count above ~400 is often inflated by counting multi-ply yarn strands individually; fabric quality is driven by staple length, yarn fineness, and weave — not the number alone.

cards.name: Warp and weft
cards.description: Warp threads run lengthwise along the loom; weft threads are woven across them horizontally. All woven fabric is built by interlacing these two. The ratio of warp to weft, and how they cross, determines the weave structure, surface character, and strength of the finished cloth. Understanding warp and weft lets you interpret every weave description a vendor gives you.
cards.example: A vendor describes a fabric as 'warp-faced.' You now understand that means more warp threads appear on the surface — producing the elongated, sometimes lustrous look of a twill or satin, rather than the balanced, matte texture of a plain weave.
cards.q: What determines whether a fabric is warp-faced or weft-faced?
cards.a: Which set of threads is more visible on the surface — warp (lengthwise) or weft (crosswise). Warp-faced fabrics show more warp on the outside; weft-faced fabrics expose more weft, as in sateen.

cards.name: Plain weave
cards.description: The simplest weave structure: each weft thread passes over one warp then under one, alternating across the width. It creates a balanced, durable fabric with a flat, matte surface. Poplin, muslin, voile, and canvas are all plain weaves. Because every thread is locked in place at every crossing, plain weave is the most durable interlacing structure — nothing floats, nothing snags.
cards.example: You want an everyday organic cotton that wears honestly and lasts years. A vendor recommends plain weave poplin: the 1/1 interlacing holds its shape, wears evenly, and sews cleanly — no floating threads to catch or abrade.
cards.q: Why is plain weave the most durable interlacing structure?
cards.a: Every thread crosses over and under each adjacent thread — nothing floats free. That locks every yarn in place, leaving no exposed surface threads to catch, snag, or wear thin.

cards.name: Percale
cards.description: A tight, balanced plain weave typically with a thread count of 200 or above. Percale has a crisp, cool, matte hand — clean and fresh rather than silky. It requires longer-staple cotton to weave tightly without roughness. Quality percale becomes noticeably softer with washing and holds its structure for years, making it the preferred choice for durable bedding and structured garments.
cards.example: A vendor offers two organic cotton options for bedding: percale and sateen. You want something that softens with age and remains crisp. You choose percale — it ages better, breathes more, and the matte surface is more forgiving of wear than sateen's floating threads.
cards.q: What hand feel and durability does percale offer compared to sateen?
cards.a: Percale is crisp, cool, and matte; it ages well and softens with washing. Sateen's long surface floats feel silkier upfront but are more prone to snagging and wear over time.

cards.name: Sateen
cards.description: A weave where weft threads float over four or more warp threads before tucking under one, creating a smooth, lustrous surface. Cotton sateen feels silky and drapes beautifully but the long exposed floats are more prone to snagging, pilling, and wear than percale. Often confused with satin, which uses the same structure but in silk or synthetic yarns.
cards.example: A vendor's sateen photographs as luxurious and commands attention in product shots. You note in your product copy that it suits occasional or decorative use — for heavy-use everyday bedding, the long weft floats will catch and wear faster than a percale of equivalent yarn quality.
cards.q: What is the trade-off of sateen's smooth surface?
cards.a: The long weft floats that create the silky look are exposed to friction — sateen snags and wears faster than percale, despite feeling more luxurious upfront.

cards.name: Selvedge
cards.description: The finished, self-bound edge running the full length of a woven fabric, formed when the weft thread loops back at each side of the loom. A clean, evenly woven selvedge signals consistent loom tension and construction quality across the full width. Inspecting the selvedge on a swatch tells you something about the standard of the production run before you cut into the body of the fabric.
cards.example: You examine swatches from two mills. One has a tight, even selvedge with consistent weave density to the very edge; the other is loose and irregular. The irregular selvedge often correlates with tension variation across the loom width — worth checking the body fabric more carefully.
cards.q: What does the selvedge tell you about fabric quality?
cards.a: A clean, tight selvedge signals consistent loom tension and construction; a loose or irregular selvedge can indicate tension problems that may affect the broader fabric quality.

cards.name: Greige goods
cards.description: Fabric as it comes directly off the loom, before any washing, bleaching, dyeing, or finishing. It is typically stiff, slightly yellowed, and contains natural waxes, plant oils, and loom-applied sizing agents. Greige is the starting material for all processing. If a vendor quotes greige fabric at a temptingly low price, remember: desizing, washing, dyeing, and finishing will add significant cost — and time.
cards.example: A mill quotes greige linen at an attractive price. You understand this is the raw, unfinished state — you will need to budget separately for desizing, washing, possible bleaching, dyeing, and finishing before the fabric is usable. The total cost is substantially higher than the greige quote.
cards.q: What does it mean when a mill quotes fabric as greige?
cards.a: Greige is fabric as it left the loom — raw, unwashed, and unfinished. All bleaching, dyeing, and finishing costs are additional and still ahead of you.

cards.name: Sizing & desizing
cards.description: Sizing is a starch or chemical coating applied to warp yarns before weaving to strengthen them and reduce breakage on the loom. It makes greige fabric stiff and gives it a false hand feel. Desizing — removing it in the first wet processing step — reveals the fabric's true character. Always request a post-desize swatch; a fabric's real hand only shows itself after sizing is gone.
cards.example: A vendor sends a swatch that feels impressively crisp and smooth. You ask: 'Is this pre- or post-desize?' It is pre-desize — the stiffness is the sizing agent, not the fabric. You request a washed swatch to evaluate the true hand before approving sampling.
cards.q: Why should you always request a post-desize swatch from a mill?
cards.a: Sizing makes greige fabric feel deceptively stiff and smooth; the fabric's true hand and quality only reveal themselves after the sizing is removed in the first wet processing step.

cards.name: Mercerization
cards.description: Treating cotton under tension with sodium hydroxide (caustic soda), causing fibers to swell, become rounder, and reflect light more evenly. The result is increased luster, improved dye uptake, and better tensile strength. However, sodium hydroxide is a harsh industrial chemical. GOTS permits mercerization only when the mill has compliant wastewater treatment. Some chemical-free brands specify un-mercerized to avoid it entirely.
cards.example: A vendor describes a cotton as 'mercerized' and you note the sheen and richer color depth. You ask: 'Is this mill GOTS-certified including wastewater controls?' For a brand positioning on chemical-free, you may choose to specify un-mercerized to remove any caustic chemistry from your supply chain.
cards.q: What does mercerization do to cotton, and what is the chemical concern?
cards.a: It uses sodium hydroxide to swell fibers, adding luster and dye uptake — but caustic soda requires GOTS-compliant wastewater controls; chemical-free brands sometimes specify un-mercerized to avoid it entirely.

cards.name: Sanforization
cards.description: A purely mechanical pre-shrinking process: fabric is dampened, compressed against a rubber belt, and dried under tension, physically shortening the fiber structure before cutting. A sanforized fabric shrinks less than 1% after washing. No chemicals are involved — it is safe under GOTS and compatible with any chemical-free standard. Always request the residual shrinkage test result, not just the claim.
cards.example: You specify bedding fabric and ask the vendor: 'Is this sanforized, and what is the residual shrinkage?' They confirm sanforized and supply a wash test showing 0.8% lengthwise shrinkage. An unsanforized linen might shrink 4–5% on the first wash — a serious problem for cut-and-sewn products.
cards.q: What does sanforized mean, and what shrinkage can you expect from a sanforized fabric?
cards.a: Sanforization mechanically pre-shrinks fabric without chemicals; residual shrinkage should be under 1% after washing. Always request the wash test result to verify the claim.

cards.name: Enzyme wash
cards.description: Using cellulase enzymes — biological proteins — to gently break down surface fibers on cotton or linen, creating a softer, broken-in feel without harsh chemicals. It is the clean alternative to stone washing or chemical softeners. GOTS-approved cellulase formulations exist, and the process uses far less water and energy than chemical softening. Always specify 'GOTS-approved enzymes' in your purchase order.
cards.example: You want your organic linen to launch pre-softened, so customers feel the quality immediately without multiple wash cycles. You specify enzyme wash with GOTS-approved cellulase. The mill confirms they use a registered enzyme — cleaner and gentler than any chemical alternative, and compatible with your certification.
cards.q: Why is enzyme washing preferable to chemical softening for an organic fabric brand?
cards.a: Cellulase enzymes soften fabric biologically without harsh chemicals; GOTS-approved formulations are available, the process uses less water and energy, and it leaves no chemical residue in the finished fabric.

cards.name: Optical brighteners (OBAs)
cards.description: Fluorescent chemical compounds applied to fabric to make it appear whiter and brighter under UV light. They are among the first substances screened out under GOTS, OEKO-TEX Standard 100, and virtually all chemical-free standards. OBAs can irritate sensitive skin and do not wash out easily. Specify 'OBA-free' explicitly in your purchase order, and verify with UV-lamp testing on samples.
cards.example: You receive fabric samples that look brilliantly white under showroom lighting. You hold them under a UV lamp — they glow bright blue, confirming the presence of OBAs. You flag this with the mill: your PO specifies OBA-free. A new sample batch must be produced before you can proceed.
cards.q: How do you detect optical brighteners in a fabric sample?
cards.a: Hold the sample under a UV (blacklight) lamp — OBAs glow bright blue under UV. Specify 'OBA-free' in your PO and use UV testing to verify any sample claiming to be clean.

cards.name: Chlorine-free bleaching
cards.description: Natural cotton and linen are off-white to slightly yellow in their greige state. Bleaching whitens them before dyeing or for white finished goods. Chlorine bleach (sodium hypochlorite) is cheap and fast but damages fibers, leaves chemical residue, and is prohibited under GOTS and OEKO-TEX Standard 100. Hydrogen peroxide (oxygen bleaching) is the GOTS-compliant alternative. Specify it explicitly — do not assume.
cards.example: You order organic white cotton percale and write into the PO: 'Chlorine-free bleaching; hydrogen peroxide only, GOTS-compliant process.' At the sampling stage, you request the bleaching process documentation to verify — never rely on a verbal assurance for chemical compliance.
cards.q: What must you specify to ensure chemical-free whitening of cotton or linen?
cards.a: Specify 'chlorine-free, hydrogen peroxide bleaching only' in the PO. Hydrogen peroxide is GOTS-compliant; chlorine bleach is prohibited and damages fiber integrity over time.

cards.name: Azo-free dyes
cards.description: Azo compounds are the most widely used dye chemistry in textiles — cheap, versatile, and colorfast. However, certain azo dyes can break down during use or washing to release carcinogenic aromatic amines. GOTS, OEKO-TEX Standard 100, and Bluesign all prohibit dyes capable of releasing these amines. Specify 'azo-free dyes, GOTS-certified, tested to ISO 20623' — and request dye datasheets at the lab-dip stage to verify before approving bulk.
cards.example: You order colored linen across twelve colorways. Your PO states: 'All dyes must be azo-free, GOTS-certified, ISO 20623 tested.' At lab-dip approval, you request the dye datasheet for each colorway and review the test certificates before signing off.
cards.q: What is the risk with azo dyes in fabric, and how do you specify around it?
cards.a: Certain azo dyes break down to release carcinogenic amines. Specify 'azo-free, GOTS-certified dyes, ISO 20623 tested' — and request dye datasheets at lab-dip stage to verify compliance before approving bulk.

cards.name: Colorfastness
cards.description: A measure of how resistant a dyed fabric's color is to fading or transfer under specific conditions: washing, rubbing (crocking), light, and perspiration. Rated on a 1–5 scale; 4.0 or above is the standard for quality apparel and home textiles. The two most critical tests for everyday products are washing fastness and crocking fastness — ask for the actual test results, not just an assurance.
cards.example: A vendor presents a richly dyed organic linen and you ask: 'Can you provide colorfastness results for washing and crocking?' They supply AATCC results showing 4/5 washing, 3.5 dry crocking. You flag the crocking — 3.5 is borderline for a fabric that will rub against upholstery or clothing. You ask for a reformulated dye run before approving.
cards.q: What colorfastness rating should you require, and which tests matter most for everyday textile products?
cards.a: Aim for 4.0+ on the 1–5 scale. Washing fastness and crocking fastness (dry and wet rubbing) are the most critical for everyday use — always request actual test certificates, not verbal assurances.

cards.name: GOTS
cards.description: The most comprehensive certification for organic textiles, covering the entire supply chain: at least 70% certified organic fiber, plus every processing step — spinning, weaving, dyeing, finishing — must occur in GOTS-certified facilities. Chemical restrictions are extensive: azo dyes, optical brighteners, heavy metals, chlorine bleach, formaldehyde, and phthalates are all prohibited. If chemical-free processing is your commitment, GOTS is the certification to require at every link in the chain.
cards.example: A vendor claims GOTS-certified cotton. You ask: 'Is your mill also GOTS-certified, or just the fiber?' Only the fiber has a certificate — the dyehouse is not certified. You explain: GOTS requires the full chain. You cannot use the GOTS label on the finished product, and you cannot make chemical-free claims with only fiber certification.
cards.q: Why is it not enough for just the cotton fiber to be GOTS-certified?
cards.a: GOTS covers the entire supply chain — fiber origin, spinning, weaving, dyeing, and finishing must all happen in GOTS-certified facilities. A GOTS fiber processed in a non-certified mill cannot carry the label.

cards.name: OCS
cards.description: Verifies that a product contains a declared percentage of organically grown material and traces it through the supply chain (chain of custody). OCS does not regulate how the fiber is processed — it makes no claims about dyeing chemicals, wastewater treatment, or factory conditions. It is a step above an unverified label, but far weaker than GOTS for chemical-free claims. OCS 100 certifies 95%+ organic content; OCS Blended covers 5–95%.
cards.example: A vendor offers fabric with OCS 100 certification. You ask: 'What dyes and finishing agents were used?' They cannot provide GOTS-compliant dye datasheets — OCS doesn't require it. For a brand built on chemical-free production, OCS fiber origin is not enough. You need GOTS.
cards.q: A fabric is OCS-certified. Does that mean it was processed without harmful chemicals?
cards.a: No. OCS only verifies organic fiber origin and traceability. It makes no claims about processing chemicals, dyestuffs, or factory conditions. Chemical-free processing requires GOTS.

cards.name: OEKO-TEX Standard 100
cards.description: A product-level certification testing the finished fabric for over 1,000 potentially harmful substances — including heavy metals, pesticide residues, formaldehyde, pH level, and azo dyes that release carcinogenic amines. It certifies the end product is safe to use, not that the production process is clean. You can have OEKO-TEX 100 fabric that was dyed in a polluting factory, as long as the residue in the finished cloth meets the test limits. Use it alongside GOTS, not instead of it.
cards.example: A vendor says their fabric is OEKO-TEX Standard 100 certified. You respond: 'This tells me the finished fabric tests clean for residues — good. But it doesn't tell me whether your dyehouse uses chlorine bleach or manages wastewater responsibly. What process certification do you hold?'
cards.q: What does OEKO-TEX Standard 100 certify, and what does it not cover?
cards.a: It certifies the finished fabric tests below limits for 1,000+ harmful substance residues. It does not certify the production process, factory conditions, or wastewater management. GOTS or Bluesign is needed for that.

cards.name: Bluesign
cards.description: A Swiss certification focused on responsible chemical management and resource efficiency at the mill level — particularly dyehouses and wet processors. It audits chemical use, water consumption, emissions, energy efficiency, and worker safety at the factory itself. Bluesign does not certify organic fiber origin but is the strongest available credential for knowing a dyehouse operates responsibly. It pairs well with OCS or GOTS fiber certification.
cards.example: A dyehouse holds Bluesign certification. You know they have been independently audited on chemical handling, wastewater treatment, and worker safety. You source GOTS-certified organic fiber separately and have it dyed at the Bluesign mill — a clean supply chain built from two complementary certifications.
cards.q: What does Bluesign certify that OEKO-TEX Standard 100 does not?
cards.a: Bluesign certifies the manufacturing process — how chemicals are managed, water and energy used, and workers protected at the mill itself. OEKO-TEX 100 tests the finished product for residues; it says nothing about how the factory runs.

cards.name: Fairtrade cotton
cards.description: Certifies that cotton farmers received a fair, stable price for their crop and worked in decent conditions. Fairtrade is primarily a social and economic standard — it ensures farmers benefit, but it does not require organic farming or chemical-free processing. A fabric can be Fairtrade but not organic; or GOTS-certified but not Fairtrade. Many buyers seek both, and some suppliers hold both certifications.
cards.example: A supplier offers GOTS-certified organic cotton from a non-Fairtrade farm; another offers Fairtrade-only cotton at a lower price with no organic certification. For a brand claiming both ethical and clean production, you need to find a supplier who holds both — or be explicit with your customers about which credential each product carries.
cards.q: Can Fairtrade cotton and GOTS-certified organic cotton be the same product?
cards.a: Not automatically — Fairtrade is a social standard for farmer welfare; GOTS is an organic fiber and processing standard. A fabric can be one, both, or neither. Look for suppliers who carry both if you need to make both claims.

cards.name: MOQ
cards.description: The smallest quantity a mill will accept for a production run, typically expressed in metres per colourway for fabric. Quality organic mills often have MOQs of 500–3,000 metres per colourway; specialty mills may run higher minimums due to the complexity of organic certification and smaller machine runs. Knowing MOQ before you design your collection prevents painful discoveries about what you can actually afford to order.
cards.example: You design six colourways of an organic linen. The mill's MOQ is 1,000 metres per colourway — meaning a minimum commitment of 6,000 metres. You restructure to four core colourways to make the order realistic for a first season.
cards.q: Why does MOQ matter when planning a collection with multiple colourways?
cards.a: MOQ applies per colourway, not per order — six colourways at 1,000m minimum means 6,000m committed. Knowing MOQ before you design prevents committing to far more fabric than your first season can absorb.

cards.name: Lead time
cards.description: The time from confirmed order to delivery at your destination. For custom fabric (specific weight, weave, color, certifications) from an organic mill: 12–20 weeks is typical. Add several weeks for sampling and approval rounds before production starts — the production clock usually starts from your final lab-dip approval, not your initial order. Always get lead time in writing, and define when it begins.
cards.example: A vendor quotes 12-week lead time. You ask: 'Does that start from initial order or from final lab-dip approval?' From approval. You estimate two more sampling rounds — realistically you are 16–18 weeks from delivery. You adjust your launch calendar accordingly.
cards.q: When does the production lead-time clock typically start at a fabric mill?
cards.a: From final lab-dip or sample approval — not from initial order. Factor in sampling rounds before the clock starts; total time to delivery is often 4–6 weeks longer than the stated production lead time.

cards.name: Lab dip & strike off
cards.description: A lab dip is a small dye test the mill produces to match your specified colour before committing to bulk dyeing. You approve or reject it against your colour standard, evaluated under D65 daylight lighting (standard lighting to prevent metameric colour shifts). Strike off is the equivalent for printed fabric. Budget for 2–3 rounds — a rejected dip is normal practice, not a sign of a problem.
cards.example: You send the mill a Pantone reference for your signature sage. They return a lab dip — warm and slightly yellow under your studio light. You evaluate under D65 lamp: still off. You reject and request round two. Round two is approved. Only then does bulk dyeing begin.
cards.q: What is a lab dip and how many rounds should you budget for?
cards.a: A small dye test to match your colour spec before bulk production; plan for 2–3 approval rounds. Always evaluate under D65 standard lighting — different light sources cause metameric shifts that make colours look different on the production floor versus your studio.

cards.name: Bulk / counter sample
cards.description: A sample cut from the actual bulk production run — after the loom is set up at scale and the dye batch is mixed for full volume — sent for your approval before you accept the full shipment. This is the last quality checkpoint before you pay for thousands of metres of fabric. A counter sample can reveal GSM drift, colour shift, or hand feel changes from sampling. Never waive it, however pressed for time you are.
cards.example: A mill says bulk is ready and offers to skip the counter sample to save two weeks. You decline. The counter sample arrives — GSM is 8% lighter than approved specification. You request a partial remake of the affected rolls. Skipping it would have meant accepting an off-spec shipment with no recourse.
cards.q: Why should you never waive a counter sample even under time pressure?
cards.a: It is your last quality gate before the full shipment — revealing whether bulk matches approved spec in weight, colour, and hand. Once you accept without a counter sample, you lose the ability to reject off-spec production.

cards.name: FOB
cards.description: An international trade contract term (Incoterm) specifying that the seller is responsible for goods until they are loaded onto the vessel at the named origin port. From that point, the buyer bears freight, insurance, and customs risk and cost. FOB [port name] is the standard basis for most Asian fabric orders. Compare: EXW (seller's factory gate only) and CIF (seller pays freight and insurance to your destination port).
cards.example: A Chinese mill quotes 'FOB Shanghai' at $8/metre. That price covers the fabric and delivery to the Shanghai port — not international freight, not insurance, not import duties. Your landed cost (what it actually costs per metre in your warehouse) will be higher once you add all of those.
cards.q: What does an FOB price from a Chinese mill include and exclude?
cards.a: FOB includes the fabric cost and delivery to the named origin port. It excludes international freight, insurance, and import duties — which all become your cost from the moment the goods are on the ship.

cards.name: Tech pack
cards.description: The complete specification document you give to a mill — your single source of truth for what you are ordering. For fabric, a tech pack should cover: fibre composition and origin, yarn count and construction, weave structure, target GSM and tolerance, certifications required (GOTS, OCS, etc.), colourways with Pantone references, finishing specifications, colorfastness minimums, shrinkage spec, and chemical restrictions. The more precise your tech pack, the fewer sampling rounds and surprises.
cards.example: You previously had a supplier produce off-spec fabric after sending a brief email description. For the next order, you build a proper tech pack: GOTS-certified organic linen, 210 GSM ±5%, Belgian water-retted flax, hydrogen peroxide bleached, enzyme softened, azo-free reactive dyes, washing fastness ≥4.0, shrinkage ≤2%. Sampling rounds drop from four to one.
cards.q: What is the purpose of a fabric tech pack?
cards.a: It is the complete specification document you give the mill — covering fibre, construction, certifications, colour, finishing, and testing requirements. A precise tech pack reduces sampling iterations and is your legal reference if a supplier delivers off-spec goods.

cards.name: Colourway
cards.description: A single colour version of a fabric. The same construction produced in five colours equals five colourways, each requiring its own MOQ, its own lab dip approval process, and its own dye batch — effectively five separate production events. Colourway count multiplies your minimum commitment, approval effort, and sampling time. Experienced buyers launch fewer colourways than they want and expand once they know what sells.
cards.example: You design an organic linen in eight colourways. At 500m MOQ each, that is 4,000m minimum — far beyond what a first season needs. You cut to four hero colourways, test the market, and add the remainder in season two based on sell-through data.
cards.q: How does adding colourways affect your production commitment and launch risk?
cards.a: Each colourway carries its own MOQ, lab dip process, and dye batch — more colourways multiply minimum commitments and approval effort. Launch fewer, prove demand, then expand.

cards.name: Shrinkage specification
cards.description: The maximum acceptable shrinkage after washing, specified in your purchase order and verified by wash test before accepting bulk. Standard specs: within ±3% for quality apparel; within ±2% for home textiles. Linen typically shrinks 3–5% in the first wash. Get wash test data at the specification stage — before patterns are cut — because shrinkage determines seam allowances, finished dimensions, and how products behave in a customer's home.
cards.example: The mill's wash test shows 4.5% lengthwise shrinkage on your linen. Your patterns were drafted with 2% allowance. You have three options: request sanforization, redraft patterns, or specify a pre-washed finish. None of these are easy to solve after cutting 3,000 metres of fabric.
cards.q: Why must you know shrinkage figures before cutting fabric?
cards.a: Shrinkage affects finished dimensions and seam allowances — if fabric shrinks beyond your built-in allowance, finished products will be undersized after washing. Get wash test data at specification stage and build it into your patterns.

decks.name: The Nature of Things
decks.tagline: Epicurus and Lucretius on how to live without fear

cards.name: Atoms and the void
cards.description: Lucretius holds that everything which exists is either indivisible particles (atoms) or the empty space (void) they move through. There is no third thing; all we see is atoms combining, colliding, and dispersing.
cards.example: A wave, a flame, a person — each is a passing pattern of atoms in motion. Nothing is solid all the way down; even stone is mostly void with particles held in arrangement.
cards.q: What are the only two things Lucretius says truly exist?
cards.a: Atoms (indivisible particles) and the void (empty space) — everything else is just atoms arranged within the void.

cards.name: Nothing comes from nothing
cards.description: Lucretius's first principle of physics: nothing can be created out of nothing. Whatever comes to be arises from pre-existing atoms by fixed natural causes, not by divine whim.
cards.example: Crops need seed, rain, and soil; they do not spring from bare ground. That things require specific ingredients and seasons is evidence that creation runs on causes, not miracles.
cards.q: What does 'nothing comes from nothing' rule out?
cards.a: Creation from nothing by the gods — everything arises from pre-existing atoms through fixed causes, which is why things need specific ingredients.

cards.name: Nothing returns to nothing
cards.description: The mirror of the first principle: nature never annihilates anything into nothing. When a thing breaks down, its atoms disperse and recombine into new things; matter is conserved.
cards.example: A fallen tree rots and feeds the forest — its atoms become soil, fungus, and fresh growth. Destruction is only rearrangement; nothing is ever truly lost.
cards.q: For Lucretius, what happens to a thing when it is destroyed?
cards.a: Its atoms disperse and recombine into other things — nothing is annihilated into nothing; matter is conserved.

cards.name: The swerve
cards.description: Lucretius's boldest idea: as atoms fall through the void they occasionally swerve by a tiny, uncaused amount. Without it, atoms would fall in parallel and never meet — and all would be rigidly determined.
cards.example: The swerve lets atoms collide and form things, and it breaks the chain of strict cause and effect, leaving room for free will. It is his answer to fatalism.
cards.q: Why does Lucretius introduce the swerve (clinamen)?
cards.a: So atoms can collide and form things, and to break strict determinism — the tiny uncaused swerve leaves room for free will.

cards.name: A boundless universe
cards.description: Lucretius argues the universe is infinite and holds countless other worlds. Fly to its supposed edge and hurl a spear: it either flies on or is stopped — either way, there is always more beyond.
cards.example: Ours is not the only world, nor the center of anything. This dethroned humanity from the cosmic center long before modern astronomy did.
cards.q: How does Lucretius argue the universe has no edge?
cards.a: The spear thought-experiment: at any supposed boundary a thrown spear flies on or is blocked by something beyond — so there is always more; the universe is infinite.

cards.name: Simulacra
cards.description: Lucretius explains perception by thin films of atoms (simulacra) that peel continuously off objects and stream into our eyes and minds. Seeing is physical contact with these films.
cards.example: You see a friend because films shed from them reach your eyes. Dreams and visions of the dead are stray films still drifting in the air — not ghosts, just physics.
cards.q: How does Lucretius explain seeing — and 'ghosts'?
cards.a: Objects shed thin atomic films (simulacra) that enter the eye; apparitions are just stray films still drifting, not spirits.

cards.name: The mortal soul
cards.kanji: the mind is made of atoms
cards.description: For Lucretius the mind and spirit are physical — fine, fast atoms woven through the body. Because the soul is material and bound to the body, it is born, grows, sickens, and dies with it.
cards.example: Wine clouds the mind, a blow knocks it out, illness confuses it. That the mind is moved by bodily things shows it is bodily — and so cannot outlive the body.
cards.q: Why does Lucretius conclude the soul is mortal?
cards.a: It is made of fine atoms bound to the body and is affected by physical things (wine, blows, illness), so it dies when the body dies.

cards.name: Death is nothing to us
cards.description: The heart of the Epicurean consolation: since the soul dies with the body, death is not an experience we undergo. Where we are, death is not; where death is, we are not.
cards.example: You will not suffer your own death any more than you suffered before you were born. There is no dark eternity to dread — only the simple absence of sensation.
cards.q: Why does Lucretius say 'death is nothing to us'?
cards.a: Because the soul ends at death, no one is left to be harmed — where death is, we are not, so it cannot touch us.

cards.name: The mirror of the past
cards.description: Lucretius disarms the fear of death by pointing back: the endless time before your birth, when you did not exist, held no terror. The endless time after death is its mirror image — equally nothing to you.
cards.example: You feel no dread about the centuries before you were born. Since after-death nonexistence is exactly the same, dreading it is simply inconsistent.
cards.q: What is Lucretius's 'symmetry' argument against fearing death?
cards.a: Nonexistence after death mirrors the nonexistence before birth, which we don't fear — so fearing death is inconsistent.

cards.name: The fear of death
cards.description: Lucretius diagnoses the terror of death as the secret source of much human misery — greed, ambition, and cruelty are attempts to outrun mortality. Cure the fear, and the vices lose their fuel.
cards.example: People hoard wealth and claw for status partly to feel they matter against death. Remove the dread, Lucretius says, and the frantic grasping quiets.
cards.q: What does Lucretius blame much human vice and striving on?
cards.a: The buried fear of death — greed, ambition, and cruelty try to escape mortality; remove the fear and they lose their fuel.

cards.name: The indifferent gods
cards.description: Epicurus and Lucretius do not deny the gods, but hold that they live in perfect tranquility, wholly unconcerned with human affairs. They neither reward nor punish, and answer no prayers.
cards.example: Storms, plagues, and good harvests are natural events, not divine verdicts. Reading them as messages from angry or pleased gods only breeds needless fear.
cards.q: What is the Epicurean view of the gods?
cards.a: They exist but live in tranquility, indifferent to us — not intervening, rewarding, or punishing, so natural events are not divine verdicts.

cards.name: Religion as a source of evil
cards.description: Lucretius uses 'religio' for the superstitious fear of the gods, and calls it a source of great evil: 'so much wrong has religion been able to urge.' Fear of divine punishment drives cruelty and misery.
cards.example: He opens with Agamemnon sacrificing his own daughter to win fair winds — superstition demanding an atrocity. The cure is understanding nature, which dissolves the fear.
cards.q: What does Lucretius mean by 'religio,' and why attack it?
cards.a: Superstitious fear of the gods — he blames it for great evil (like Iphigenia's sacrifice); understanding nature dissolves the fear.

cards.name: A world not made for us
cards.description: Lucretius rejects the idea that the world was designed for humanity. The cosmos arose from countless atomic collisions over vast time, not a plan — as its flaws and hostility suggest.
cards.example: Deserts, disease, wild beasts, and infant helplessness are poor evidence of a world built for our benefit. Order emerged by trial across aeons, not by intention.
cards.q: Why does Lucretius deny the world was designed for us?
cards.a: It arose from atomic collisions over vast time, not a plan — and its flaws and hostility are poor evidence of benevolent design.

cards.name: Epicurus the liberator
cards.description: Lucretius casts Epicurus not as a god but as a human hero who freed mankind from the crushing fear of the gods and of death. Where religion had pinned humanity down, understanding nature broke the chains.
cards.example: He praises Epicurus as the first to raise mortal eyes against superstition and travel beyond the 'flaming walls of the world' in thought, returning with the truth that frees us.
cards.q: How does Lucretius portray Epicurus?
cards.a: As a human liberator who freed mankind from the fear of gods and death through understanding nature — the poem's hero, not a deity.

cards.name: The plague of Athens
cards.description: Lucretius ends On the Nature of Things not with comfort but with a harrowing account of the plague that devastated Athens — bodies, panic, and the collapse of custom. Nature is indifferent; it falls to us to face it clearly.
cards.example: The unflinching finale refuses false consolation. It leaves the reader with the Epicurean task: meet even catastrophe without the added torment of superstition.
cards.q: How does Lucretius end his poem, and why is it striking?
cards.a: With the brutal plague of Athens — no comforting close; nature is indifferent, and we are left to face reality clearly, without superstition.

cards.name: Pleasure as the highest good
cards.description: Epicurean 'pleasure' is not indulgence but the absence of pain in body and mind. The aim is not to chase intense sensations, which bring their own troubles, but to reach a stable, contented ease.
cards.example: A simple meal eaten with a calm mind and good company beats a lavish feast shadowed by excess and anxiety. The goal is to remove disturbance, not to pile on stimulation.
cards.q: What do the Epicureans actually mean by 'pleasure'?
cards.a: The absence of pain in body and mind — a stable contentment, not indulgence or the chasing of intense sensation.

cards.name: Ataraxia
cards.description: Ataraxia is the tranquil, unshaken mind that is the true aim of Epicurean life — a serenity reached by removing fear (of gods and death) and limiting desire. It is peace, not thrill.
cards.example: The sage is like calm water: events ripple the surface but do not churn the depths. Understanding nature is what makes this steadiness possible.
cards.q: What is ataraxia, and what produces it?
cards.a: Tranquility — an unshaken mind, produced by removing the fear of gods and death and by limiting desire.

cards.name: The fourfold cure
cards.description: The Epicurean remedy for anxiety in four strokes: do not fear the gods; do not fear death; what is good is easy to get; what is terrible is easy to endure. Memorize it, and you carry the therapy in miniature.
cards.example: Faced with dread, run the four: the gods do not threaten you, death will not be experienced, your real needs are simple, and severe pain is either brief or bearable. The panic loosens.
cards.q: What are the four parts of the Epicurean 'fourfold cure'?
cards.a: Don't fear the gods; don't fear death; what's good is easy to get; what's terrible is easy to endure.

cards.name: The three kinds of desire
cards.description: Epicurus sorts desires into three: natural and necessary (food, shelter, safety, friendship), natural but unnecessary (luxuries), and vain and empty (fame, unlimited wealth). Happiness comes from meeting the first and pruning the rest.
cards.example: Hunger is easily met with plain food; a craving for gourmet excess is optional; the hunger for fame is bottomless and never filled. Wisdom is telling them apart.
cards.q: How does Epicurus classify desires, and which should we pursue?
cards.a: Natural-and-necessary, natural-but-unnecessary, and vain/empty — satisfy the necessary (easily met) and prune the rest, especially the vain.

cards.name: The view from the shore
cards.description: Lucretius's famous image: it is sweet, from dry land, to watch a ship struggle in a storm at sea — not because you relish others' danger, but because you see clearly what troubles you are free of.
cards.example: Standing on the shore of understanding, the Epicurean watches the shipwreck of ambition, greed, and superstition below — grateful not for others' pain but for hard-won peace.
cards.q: What does Lucretius's image of watching a storm 'from the shore' represent?
cards.a: The calm of the wise: not pleasure in others' suffering, but relief and clarity at seeing what troubles one is spared.
